name: Deno Deploy API
what_it_does: Edge function hosting platform with a REST API for deploying JavaScript and TypeScript functions globally across 35+ regions using Deno runtime with zero configuration.
use_cases: Deploying TypeScript edge middleware globally to intercept and transform HTTP requests closest to users without managing regional infrastructure
Automating deployment of new edge function versions from agent-driven CI pipelines using the deployments API
Creating isolated per-customer or per-tenant edge function deployments programmatically for multi-tenant agent architectures
Running globally distributed A/B testing logic or feature flag evaluation at the edge, deployed and updated via API
Hosting lightweight agent webhook receivers at low latency globally by programmatically creating new Deno Deploy projects
not_for: Long-running background jobs or batch processing — Deno Deploy functions are stateless and have strict CPU time limits per request
Applications requiring native Node.js modules, file system access, or persistent local state beyond KV storage
Teams whose codebase is tightly coupled to Node.js-specific APIs that are incompatible with the Deno runtime
best_when: An agent needs to deploy globally distributed TypeScript functions at the edge with sub-millisecond cold starts and zero infrastructure management, particularly for latency-sensitive request handling.
avoid_when: Your workload requires long-running processes, heavy compute, Node.js native modules, or a non-TypeScript/JavaScript runtime.

auth.notes: Personal access tokens generated from Deno Deploy dashboard. Passed as Bearer headers. Organization tokens available for team access. No per-project or per-operation scope granularity — tokens have full account access. Token expiry is not enforced by default.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free: 100,000 requests/day, 100 GiB outbound bandwidth/month, 1ms CPU time per request
pricing.paid_tiers: Pro: $20/month — 5 million requests/month included, higher CPU time limits, custom domains, SLA; Enterprise: custom pricing
pricing.notes: Free tier is sufficient for development and low-traffic deployments. Pro plan required for SLA guarantees and production workloads. CPU time limits (not wall-clock time) can be a surprising constraint for compute-heavy functions.

performance.rate_limits: Free: 100K requests/day; 1ms CPU time/request. Pro: 5M requests/month; 50ms CPU time/request. API management endpoints: no published rate limits.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Deployment API returns structured JSON errors with descriptive messages. Runtime errors from deployed functions are available through the deployment logs API. Error codes are documented for common failure cases. Build/bundling errors include the specific line and file causing the failure.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Deployment creation is not idempotent — each call creates a new deployment. Project creation will error if the project name already exists, enabling safe existence checks. No idempotency key support. Deployments are immutable once created.
known_agent_gotchas: CPU time limits (1ms free, 50ms pro) are measured in actual CPU cycles, not wall-clock time — I/O-bound functions appear to run longer but only burn CPU during compute, which can be confusing when diagnosing timeouts
Deployments are immutable and cannot be modified after creation — agents must create a new deployment for every code change, which generates many deployment objects over time
Custom domains require DNS verification that cannot be fully automated via API alone — agents cannot provision custom domains end-to-end without human DNS intervention
The Deno Deploy API does not support webhooks for deployment events — agents must poll deployment status endpoints to determine when a deployment becomes live
Deno module imports use URL-based ESM — npm package compatibility is via the npm: specifier, but not all npm packages work correctly in the Deno runtime, requiring testing before automation

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all API and edge function endpoints. Environment variables/secrets are stored encrypted and injected at runtime. No per-project scope on API tokens — full account access with any token. Deno's permission model provides runtime sandboxing for deployed functions. No published compliance certifications.
